title: Cat Walking on Three Legs: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat limping? Learn why cats walk on three legs, when to seek veterinary care, and how photos help with a quick triage assessment.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat walking on three legs is experiencing significant pain and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if the limb is at an unnatural angle, there is heavy bleeding, or your cat is lethargic.

Why is my cat suddenly walking on three legs?

When a cat suddenly starts walking on three legs, it is a clear sign of significant discomfort or pain. This behavior, known as lameness, suggests that the cat is unable or unwilling to put weight on a specific limb. Common causes include:

  • Paw pad injuries
  • Broken nails
  • Sprains or fractures
  • Painful abscesses resulting from bite wounds

Is my cat's limping a medical emergency?

The urgency level for a cat walking on three legs is categorized as Medium. While it may not be an immediate life-threatening crisis, it indicates your cat is in pain and requires medical attention. You should follow these guidelines:

  • Aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the leg is dangling at an unnatural angle.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if there is heavy bleeding.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat seems extremely lethargic.

How do photos and videos help a veterinarian triage my cat?

Capturing a clear photo or a short video of your cat attempting to walk can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Visuals allow a professional to provide a more accurate assessment by:

  • Evaluating the degree of weight-bearing on the limb.
  • Identifying visible swelling, lesions, or wounds.
  • Observing the specific gait and movement patterns.
  • Determining the urgency of the visit and preparing necessary diagnostic tools in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lameness in cats, presenting as a three-legged gait, requires a thorough clinical examination to determine the underlying cause, as treatment varies accordingly. The lameness examination is critical to identify musculoskeletal lesions, and evaluation should be performed with the animal at rest, rising, and during locomotion on flat or inclined surfaces, noting single- or multiple-limb involvement. Useful ancillary tests include radiography, ultrasonography, arthrocentesis, arthroscopy, arthrography, electromyography, and tissue biopsy with histopathology. Advanced imaging techniques, including bone scans, CT, and MRI, are increasingly used in specialty clinics and university hospitals for subtle lesions.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat human pain medications like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol. These are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Should I try to wrap the leg myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally best not to wrap the leg at home. Improper bandaging can cut off circulation or cause further pain. Keep your cat in a small, confined area like a carrier or a small room to limit their movement until you can get to the vet.

faqs.question: What if my cat is still eating and acting normally otherwise?
faqs.answer: Cats are masters at hiding pain. Even if they are eating, walking on three legs is a definitive sign of a problem that needs a professional diagnosis to prevent long-term damage or chronic pain.
